1.2: The leaders of all the people ; literally : the angles of the people ; Those who were like the cornerstones that supported the entire edifice of the nation. This metaphor is often used in Scripture.
1.8: . ― Lebona , a town north of Silo.

1.10: ). A strong iron bar, hooked at one end, is suspended from a heavy ring of the same metal, fixed to a sturdy post firmly embedded in the wall on either side of the gate. When the gate is closed, the hook of the flying buttresses engages in an iron ring attached behind each leaf, so that the gate can withstand considerable pressure from the outside. The lock is massive, of wrought iron, and the long-handled key, very heavy, is worn on the belt by the gatekeeper or hung on a nail in the small apartment nearby. It took the strength of Samson to pull the gates of Gaza from their hinges, with both posts, bar, and everything, and carry them to the top of the hill overlooking Hebron. 1.40: Contrary to the opinion of all the ancients, several modern scholars claim that Jephthah's daughter was not actually sacrificed, but merely consecrated to the service of the sanctuary. Whatever opinion one adopts, nothing can be concluded against the divinity of the Hebrew religion. For Jephthah's vow is an entirely personal act. It was not commanded by law, since the law, on the contrary, so expressly forbade the sacrifice of human victims. 2.1: The angel of the Lord went up from Gilgal to Bochim and said, «I brought you up out of Egypt and led you into the land I swore to your ancestors to give you. I said, ‘I will never break my covenant with you,’”,
2.2: And you will not make a treaty with the inhabitants of this land; you will tear down their altars. But you have not obeyed my voice. Why have you done this?
2.3: And I too said: I will not drive them out before you; they will be at your side, and their gods will be a snare to you.»
2.4: As the angel of the Lord spoke these words to all the children of Israel, the people lifted up their voices and wept.
2.5: They named the place Bokim and offered sacrifices to the Lord there.
2.6: Joshua sent the people away, and the children of Israel each went to their inheritance to take possession of the land.
2.7: The people served the Lord throughout the life of Joshua and throughout the lives of the elders who outlived Joshua and who had seen all the great work that the Lord had accomplished in favor of Israel.
2.8: Joshua, son of Nun, servant of the Lord, died at the age of one hundred and ten years.
2.9: He was buried in the territory he had been allotted, at Tamnath-Heres, in the hill country of Ephraim, north of Mount Gaas.
2.10: This whole generation was also gathered to their fathers, and after them another generation arose who did not know the Lord, nor the work that he had done for Israel.
2.11: The children of Israel did what was evil in the sight of the Lord and they served the Baals.
2.12: They abandoned the Lord, the God of their fathers, who had brought them out of the land of Egypt, and they went after other gods, from among the gods of the peoples who surrounded them, they bowed down before them and they angered the Lord.
2.13: Abandoning the Lord, they served Baal and the Astartes.
2.14: The Lord’s anger burned against Israel, he delivered them into the hands of plunderers who plundered them, and he sold them into the hands of their enemies all around, and they could no longer stand before their enemies.
2.15: Wherever they went, the hand of the Lord was against them for their harm, as the Lord had said, as the Lord had sworn to them, and they came to great distress.
2.16: The Lord raised up judges who delivered them from the hand of those who plundered them.
2.17: But they did not listen to their judges, for they prostituted themselves to other gods and bowed down to them. They quickly turned away from the way their fathers had followed in obeying the commandments of the Lord; they did not do the same.
2.18: When the Lord raised up judges for them, the Lord was with the judge and delivered them from the hand of their enemies, as long as the judge lived, for the Lord relented because of their groaning before those who oppressed and tormented them.
2.19: But, after the death of the judge, they became even more corrupt than their fathers, going after other gods to serve them and prostrate themselves before them; they did not abandon their errors and their obstinacy.
2.20: Then the Lord’s anger burned against Israel, and he said, «Because this nation has transgressed my covenant that I commanded their ancestors, and because they have not obeyed my voice,
2.21: I will no longer drive out before them a single one of the nations that Joshua left behind when he died,
2.22: "To test Israel through them, to see whether they will be careful to follow the way of the Lord, as their ancestors were careful."»
2.23: And the Lord left in peace, without hastening to drive them out, those nations whom he had not delivered into the hands of Joshua.

3.1: These are the nations that the Lord left in peace, to test Israel through them, all those who had not experienced all the wars of Canaan,
3.2: and this solely for the instruction of future generations of Israel, to teach them about war, at least those who had not experienced it before.
3.3: These nations were: the five princes of the Philistines, all the Canaanites and the Sidonians and the Hivites who inhabited the mountain of Lebanon, from the mountain of Baal-Hermon to the entrance of Hamath.
3.4: These peoples were to be used to test Israel, to see if they would obey the commandments that the Lord had prescribed to their fathers through Moses.
3.5: And the children of Israel dwelt among the Canaanites, the Hittites, the Amorites, the Perizzites, the Hivites, and the Jebusites,
3.6: They took their daughters as wives and gave their own daughters to their sons, and they served their gods.
3.7: The children of Israel did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, forgetting the Lord, they served the Baals and the Asheroths.
3.8: The Lord’s anger was kindled against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of Chusan-Rasathaim, king of Mesopotamia, and the children of Israel were enslaved to Chusan-Rasathaim for eight years.
3.9: The children of Israel cried out to the Lord, and the Lord raised up a deliverer for them, Othniel, son of Kenez, the younger brother of Caleb.
3.10: The Spirit of the Lord came upon him, he judged Israel and went out to war, the Lord delivered into his hand Chusan-Rasathaim, king of Mesopotamia, and his hand prevailed against Chusan-Rasathaim.
3.11: The country was at rest for forty years and Othoniel, son of Cénez, died.
3.12: The children of Israel again did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, and the Lord strengthened Eglon, king of Moab, against Israel, because they did what was evil in the sight of the Lord.
3.13: Eglon gathered the sons of Ammon and Amalek and marched out. He defeated Israel and they captured the city of Palm Trees.
3.14: The children of Israel were enslaved for eighteen years to Eglon, king of Moab.
3.15: The Israelites cried out to the Lord, and the Lord raised up a deliverer for them, Ehud son of Gera, a Benjamite, who was left-handed. The Israelites sent a present through him to Eglon king of Moab.
3.16: Aod made himself a double-edged sword, a cubit long, and he girded it under his clothes, on his right hip.
3.17: He offered the gift to Eglon, king of Moab, now Eglon was a very fat man.
3.18: When he had finished offering the gift, he sent away the people who had brought the gift.
3.19: And he himself returned from the idols that are near Gilgal and said, "O king, I have something secret to tell you." The king said, "Silence." And all those who were with him came out.
3.20: Aod approached him as he sat alone in his summer room and said, "I have a word from God for you." Eglon rose from his seat.
3.21: Then Aod, extending his left hand, pulled out the sword that was on his right hip and plunged it into his stomach.
3.22: The handle itself went in after the blade and the grease closed over the blade, because he did not remove the sword from his belly and the blade came out from behind.
3.23: Aod went out by the outside staircase, closed the doors of the upper room on Eglon and pulled the bolt.
3.24: When he had gone out, the king's servants came and looked, and behold, the doors of the upper room were bolted. They said, "No doubt he is covering his feet in the summer room."«
3.25: They waited a long time until they were ashamed, and when he did not open the doors of the upper room, they took the key and opened it, and there was their master lying on the floor, lifeless.
3.26: During their delay, Aod fled, passed the idols and escaped to Seirath.
3.27: As soon as he arrived, he sounded the trumpet in the mountains of Ephraim. The Israelites came down with him from the mountains, and he led them.
3.28: He said to them, «Follow me, for the Lord has delivered your enemies, the Moabites, into your hands.» So they went down after him, seized the fords of the Jordan opposite Moab, and let no one pass through.
3.29: They defeated Moab at that time, about ten thousand men, all strong and valiant, and not one escaped.
3.30: On that day, Moab was humbled under the hand of Israel, and the land had rest for eighty years.
3.31: After him came Samgar, son of Anath, who defeated six hundred Philistine men with an ox goad; he too was a deliverer of Israel.

4.1: The children of Israel did again what was evil in the sight of the Lord, after the death of Ehud.
4.2: And the Lord sold them into the hands of Jabin, king of Canaan, who reigned in Ashor, the commander of his army was Sisera and he lived in Haroseth-Goyim.
4.3: The children of Israel cried out to the Lord, for Jabin had nine hundred iron chariots and for twenty years he had been oppressing the children of Israel severely.
4.4: At that time, Deborah, a prophetess, the wife of Lapidoth, was administering justice in Israel.
4.5: She sat under the palm tree of Deborah, between Ramah and Bethel, in the hill country of Ephraim, and the children of Israel went up to her to be judged.
4.6: She sent for Barak, son of Abinoëm, from Kedes in Naphtali, and said to him, «Is this not the command given by the Lord, the God of Israel? Go, go to Mount Tabor and take with you ten thousand men from the sons of Naphtali and the sons of Zebulun.
4.7: I will bring Sisera, the commander of Jabin’s army, with his chariots and troops, to the Wadi Zishon, and I will deliver him into your hands.»
4.8: Barak said to her, «If you come with me, I will go; but if you do not come with me, I will not go.»
4.9: She replied, «Yes, I will go with you, but in the expedition you are about to undertake, the glory will not be yours, for the Lord will deliver Sisera into the hands of a woman.» So Deborah arose and went with Barak to Cedes.
4.10: Barak summoned Zebulun and Naphtali to Cedes, and ten thousand men went out after him, and Deborah went with him.
4.11: Heber the Kinnean had captured the Kinneans, the sons of Hobab, Moses' brother-in-law, and had pitched his tent as far as the oak of Sennim, near Kedesh.
4.12: Sisera was informed that Barak, son of Abinoëm, had gone to Mount Tabor,
4.13: And Sisera brought from Haroseth-goyim, to the Wadi Zishon, all his chariots, nine hundred iron chariots, and all the people who were with him.
4.14: Then Deborah said to Barak, «Get up, for this is the day the Lord has given Sisera into your hands. Has not the Lord gone out before you?» And Barak went down from Mount Tabor, with ten thousand men following him.
4.15: The Lord routed Sisera, all his chariots and all his army, with the edge of the sword, before Barak, and Sisera got down from his chariot and fled on foot.
4.16: Barak pursued the chariots and the army to Haroseth-goyim and all of Sisera's army fell by the edge of the sword, not a man escaped.
4.17: Sisera fled on foot to the tent of Jahel, wife of Heber the Kinnee, for there was peace between Jabin king of Ashor and the house of Heber the Kinnee.
4.18: Jahel went out to meet Sisara and said to him, «Come in, my lord, come in to me, do not be afraid.» He went into her tent and she hid him under a covering.
4.19: He said to her, «Please give me a little water to drink, for I am thirsty.» She opened the skin of milk, gave him a drink, and covered him.
4.20: He told him, «Stand at the entrance of the tent, and if anyone comes to ask him, »Is there a man here?’ you shall answer, ‘No.’”
4.21: Jahel, Heber's wife, took a tent peg and, having taken the hammer in her hand, she approached him gently and drove the peg into his temple, which penetrated into the ground, for he was sleeping soundly, being overcome with fatigue, and he died.
4.22: And behold, as Barak pursued Sisara, Jahel went out to meet him and said to him, «Come, and I will show you the man you seek.» He went into her house and saw Sisara lying dead with a stake through his temple.
4.23: On that day, God humiliated Jabin, king of Canaan, before the children of Israel.
4.24: And the hand of the children of Israel pressed harder and harder on Jabin, king of Canaan, until they had destroyed Jabin, king of Canaan.

5.1: On that day, Deborah and Barak, sons of Abinoëm, sang, saying:
5.2: The leaders took the lead in Israel, the people willingly offered themselves for the fight, bless the Lord for it.
5.3: Listen, O kings: princes, give ear. It is I, it is I who will sing to the Lord, I will sing a song to the Lord, the God of Israel.
5.4: Lord, when you went out from Seir, when you advanced from the fields of Edom, the earth trembled, the heavens themselves melted, and the clouds melted into water.
5.5: Before the Lord the mountains trembled, this Sinai, before the Lord, the God of Israel.
5.6: In the days of Samgar, son of Anath, in the days of Jahel, the roads were deserted and travelers took winding paths.
5.7: The countryside was neglected in Israel, until I, Deborah, rose up, a mother in Israel.
5.8: New gods were chosen, so war was at the gates and neither shield nor spear was seen among forty thousand in Israel.
5.9: My heart goes out to the leaders of Israel, to those of the people who have offered themselves: Bless the Lord.
5.10: You who ride white donkeys, who sit on carpets and you who travel the roads, sing.
5.11: Let the archers, by the watering places, proclaim with their voices the righteous acts of the Lord, the righteous acts he has performed in Israel. Then the people of the Lord came down into his gates.
5.12: Awake, awake, Deborah. Awake, awake, sing the song. Rise up, Barak, and take your prisoners, sons of Abinoëm.
5.13: Descend now, you remaining nobles of the people. Lord, descend to me among these heroes.
5.14: From Ephraim came those whose roots are in Amalek, behind you Benjamin joined your troops, from Machir came leaders, from Zebulun commanders, with the scribe's staff.
5.15: The princes of Issachar are with Deborah, Issachar is beside Barak, in the plain he is sent after him. Near the streams of Reuben, there were great resolutions of the heart:
5.16: Why did you remain in the middle of your pastures, listening to the pipe of your shepherds? By the streams of Reuben, there were great resolutions of the heart.
5.17: Gilead did not leave his home beyond the Jordan, and Dan remained in his ships; Asher stayed put on the seashore and remained in his harbors.
5.18: But Zebulun is a people who expose their soul to death, as is Naphtali, on his high plateaus.
5.19: The kings came, they fought, so they fought, the kings of Canaan, at Thanac, by the waters of Mageddo, they did not win a single ingot of silver.
5.20: From the heavens they fought for us, from their paths the stars fought against Sisera.
5.21: The Cison torrent has carried away their corpses, the torrent of olden times, the Cison torrent. O my soul, advance boldly.
5.22: Then the hooves of the horses resounded, in the race, the swift race of their warriors.
5.23: "Curse Meroz," said the angel of the Lord, "curse, curse its inhabitants, for they did not come to the aid of the Lord, to the aid of the Lord, with the mighty ones.".
5.24: Blessed be Jahel, wife of Heber the Kyinean, among the troops who dwell in the tent, blessed be she.
5.25: He asked for water, she gave milk; in the cup of honor, she offered the purest milk.
5.26: With one hand she seized the stake and with her right, the workman's hammer. She struck Sisara, she crushed his head, she shattered and pierced his temple,
5.27: At his feet, he sags, he falls, he is stretched out; at his feet he sags, he falls: where he sags, there he lies lifeless.
5.28: Through the window, through the latticework, she looks at Sisara's mother and cries out: "Why is his chariot delayed? Why is the movement of his chariots so slow?"«
5.29: The wisest of her ladies answered her, and she repeated their words to herself:
5.30: «Have they not found the spoils? Have they not divided them among themselves? One young girl, two young girls for each warrior, colored garments as spoils for Sisara, garments of various colors as spoils, one colored garment, two garments of various colors, for the shoulders of the wife.»
5.31: May all your enemies perish, O Lord. And may those who love him be like the sun when it rises in its strength.
5.32: The country was at rest for forty years.

6.1: The children of Israel did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, and the Lord delivered them into the hands of Midian for seven years.
6.2: The hand of Midian was powerful against Israel. Because of Midian, the children of Israel made crevices, caves, and steep places in the mountains.
6.3: When Israel had sown, Midian came up with Amalek and the sons of the East and they marched against him.
6.4: Encamped opposite Israel, they devastated the land's produce as far as Gaza and left no sustenance in Israel, neither sheep, nor oxen, nor donkeys.
6.5: For they came up with their flocks and their tents, and they came in great numbers like swarms of locusts; they and their camels were countless, and they came into the land to devastate it.
6.6: Israel was greatly weakened because of Midian, and the children of Israel cried out to the Lord.
6.7: When the Israelites cried out to the Lord about Midian,
6.8: The Lord sent a prophet to the children of Israel. He said to them, «Thus says the Lord, the God of Israel: I brought you up out of Egypt and brought you out of the house of bondage.
6.9: I delivered you from the hand of the Egyptians and from the hand of all your oppressors; I drove them out before you and gave you their land.
6.10: I told you, »I am the Lord your God; you shall not fear the gods of the Amorites, in whose land you dwell.” But you did not listen to my voice.»
6.11: And the angel of the Lord came and sat under the oak in Ephraia, who would bring to Joash of the family of Abiezer. Gideon, his son, was threshing wheat in the winepress to hide it from the Midianites.
6.12: The angel of the Lord appeared to him and said, «The Lord is with you, mighty warrior.»
6.13: Gideon said to him, «Ah, my lord, if the Lord is with us, why has all this happened to us? And where are all his wonders that our fathers told us about, saying, »Did not the Lord bring us up out of Egypt?’ But now the Lord has abandoned us and given us into the hands of Midian.”
6.14: The Lord turned to him and said, «Go in the strength you have and deliver Israel from the hand of Midian. Have I not sent you?»
6.15: Gideon said to him, «Ah, Lord, how can I deliver Israel? Look, my clan is the poorest in Manasseh, and I am the least in my father’s house.»
6.16: The Lord said to him, «I will be with you, and you will strike down the Midianites as one man.»
6.17: Gideon said to him, "If I have found favor in your eyes, give me a sign that it is you who are speaking to me.
6.18: »Do not leave this place until I return to you and bring my offering and set it before you.« And the Lord said, »I will stay until you return.”
6.19: Gideon, having entered, prepared a young goat and, with an ephah of flour, he made unleavened loaves, then, having put the meat in a basket and the juice in a vessel, he brought them to him under the terebinth tree and offered them to him.
6.20: The Angel of the Lord said to him, «Take the meat and the unleavened bread, place them on this rock and pour over the juice.» And he did so.
6.21: The angel of the Lord stretched out the tip of the staff that was in his hand and touched the meat and the unleavened bread. Immediately fire sprang up from the rock and consumed the meat and the unleavened bread, and the angel of the Lord vanished from his sight.
6.22: Gideon saw that it was the Angel of the Lord, and Gideon said, «Woe to me, Lord God, for I have seen the Angel of the Lord face to face!»
6.23: The Lord said to him, «Be at peace, do not be afraid, you will not die.»
6.24: Gideon built an altar there to the Lord and called it: "the Lord-Shalom," this altar still exists today in Ephrah of Abieser.
6.25: That night, the Lord said to Gideon, «Take your father’s young bull and the second bull, seven years old. Tear down the altar of Baal that belongs to your father and cut down the Asherah that is beside it.
6.26: Then you shall build an altar to the Lord your God on the top of this stronghold and set it up. You shall take the second bull and offer it as a burnt offering with the wood of the Asherah tree that you have cut down.»
6.27: Gideon took ten men from among his servants and carried out what the Lord had told him: but, as he did not dare to do it by day for fear of his father's house and the people of the city, he did it by night.
6.28: When the people of the city got up the next morning, behold, the altar of Baal was overturned, the Asherah which was beside it was cut down, and the second bull was being offered as a burnt offering on the newly built altar.
6.29: They asked each other, "Who did this?" and they inquired and made some inquiries. They were told, "Gideon, son of Joash, did this."«
6.30: Then the people of the city said to Joash, «Bring out your son so that he may die, for he has torn down the altar of Baal and cut down the Asherah that was beside it.»
6.31: Joash said to all those who stood against him, «Are you going to take Baal’s side? Or are you going to help him? Whoever takes Baal’s side shall be put to death before morning. If Baal is God, let him take up his own cause, since his altar has been torn down.»
6.32: On that day Gideon was given the name Jerobaal, and they said, "Let Baal defend himself against him, since he has torn down his altar."«
6.33: All Midian, Amalek, and the people of the East gathered together and, having crossed the Jordan, they camped in the plain of Jezreel.
6.34: The Spirit of the Lord came upon Gideon, and he sounded the trumpet, and the Abieserites gathered to follow him.
6.35: He sent messengers throughout Manasseh, and they also gathered to follow him. He sent messengers to Asher, Zebulun, and Naphtali, and they went up to meet them.
6.36: Gideon said to God, «If you want to save Israel by my hand, as you have said
6.37: »See, I will put a fleece of wool on the threshing floor. If only the fleece is covered with dew, and all the surrounding ground is dry, then I will know that you will deliver Israel by my hand, as you have said.”
6.38: And so it happened. The next day, having risen early in the morning, he squeezed the fleece and brought out the dew, filling a cup with water.
6.39: Gideon said to God, "Do not let your anger burn against me, so that I may speak just once more: I would like to test the fleece only once more: let the fleece alone remain dry and let the dew fall on all the surrounding ground."«
6.40: And God did this that night: only the fleece remained dry, and all the ground was covered with dew.

7.1: Jerobaal, who is Gideon, with all the people who were with him, got up early in the morning and went to camp above the spring of Harad. The camp of Midian was north of Gideon's camp, towards the hill of Moreh, in the plain.
7.2: The Lord said to Gideon, «The people you have with you are too many for me to give Midian into their hands, lest Israel boast against me, saying, ‘It was my hand that delivered me.’”.
7.3: "Therefore proclaim this to the people: 'Whoever is afraid and trembling should return and depart from Mount Gilboa.'" Twenty-two thousand men of the people returned, and ten thousand remained.
7.4: The Lord said to Gideon, «The people are still too many. Bring them down to the water, and there I will separate them for you. Those I tell you to go with you will go with you, and those I tell you not to go with you will not go with you.»
7.5: Gideon brought the people down to the water, and the Lord said to Gideon, «All those who lap the water with their tongues, like a dog, must be separated, as well as all those who kneel down to drink.»
7.6: Those who lapped the water in their hands, bringing it to their mouths, numbered three hundred men; all the rest of the people had knelt down to drink.
7.7: And the Lord said to Gideon, «It is with these three hundred men who have lapped that I will save you and deliver Midian into your hands; let all the rest of the people go home.»
7.8: The three hundred took food from the people and their trumpets, then Gideon sent all the rest of the men of Israel back to their tents, keeping the three hundred men. The camp of the Midianites was below him in the plain.
7.9: During that night, the Lord said to Gideon, «Get up, go down to the camp, for I have given it into your hands.
7.10: If you fear to attack him, go down with Pharaoh, your servant,
7.11: "You will listen to what they say, and then your hands will be strengthened, and you will go down to the camp without fear." He went down with Phara, his servant, to the outposts of the camp.
7.12: Midian, Amalek, and all the sons of the East were spread out in the plain, as numerous as locusts, and their camels were without number, like the sand on the seashore.
7.13: Gideon arrived, and behold, a man was telling his companion a dream, saying, «I had a dream, and behold: a barley loaf rolled into the camp of Midian; it came to the tent, struck it, and it fell; it overturned it, and the tent fell.»
7.14: His companion replied, "It is none other than the sword of Gideon, son of Joash, a man of Israel, with which God delivered Midian and the entire camp."«
7.15: When Gideon heard the account of the dream and its interpretation, he bowed down and, returning to the camp of Israel, said, «Arise, for the Lord has delivered the camp of Midian into our hands.»
7.16: He divided the three hundred men into three columns and gave each of them trumpets and empty jugs, with torches inside the jugs.
7.17: And he said to them, «You will watch me and do as I do. As soon as I arrive at the edge of the camp, you will do what I do.”.
7.18: When I and all who are with me sound the trumpet, you also shall sound the trumpet all around the camp and say, »For the Lord and for Gideon!”
7.19: Gideon and the hundred men who were with him arrived at the edge of the camp at the beginning of the middle watch, as the sentries had just been relieved, they blew the trumpet and broke the jugs that were in their hands.
7.20: Then the three bodies sounded the trumpets and broke the jugs, and, taking the torches in their left hand and the trumpets in their right to sound them, they cried out: "A sword for the Lord and for Gideon!"«
7.21: They each stayed in their place around the camp and the whole camp began to run, shout and flee.
7.22: While the three hundred men were blowing the trumpets, the Lord caused the Midianites to turn their swords against each other and against the whole camp. The camp fled as far as Beth-setta toward Zarerah, to the border of Abel-mehula, near Tebbath.
7.23: The men of Israel gathered together, those of Naphtali, Asher, and all Manasseh, and they pursued Midian.
7.24: Gideon sent messengers throughout the hill country of Ephraim, saying, «Go down to meet the Midianites and occupy the fords of the Jordan before them, as far as Bethbera.» So all the men of Ephraim gathered together and occupied the fords of the Jordan as far as Bethbera.
7.25: Having captured two princes of Midian, Oreb and Zeb, they killed Oreb at the rock of Oreb and they killed Zeb at the winepress of Zeb. They pursued Midian and brought the heads of Oreb and Zeb to Gideon, on the other side of the Jordan.

8.1: The men of Ephraim said to Gideon, "What is this you have done to us, not calling us when you went out to fight against Midian?" And they quarreled with him fiercely.
8.2: Gideon answered them, «What have I done compared to you? Is not the gleaning of Ephraim better than the vintage of Abiezer?
8.3: "It is into your hands that the Lord has delivered the princes of Midian, Oreb and Zeb. What could I do compared to you?" When he had said this, their anger against him subsided.
8.4: Gideon arrived at the Jordan and crossed it, he and the three hundred men who were with him, tired and continuing to pursue.
8.5: He said to the people of Soccoth, "Please give some loaves of bread to the people who are with me, for they are tired and I am pursuing Zeba and Shalman, kings of Midian."«
8.6: The leaders of Soccoth replied, "Is the hand of Zebae and Shalman already in yours, that we should give bread to your troops?"«
8.7: Gideon said to them, «Well, when the Lord has delivered Zeba and Salmana into my hands, I will tear your flesh with desert thorns and thistles.»
8.8: From there he went up to Phanuel and addressed the people of Phanuel with the same message. The people of Phanuel answered him as the people of Soccoth had answered.
8.9: And he said to Phanuel's people, "When I return victorious, I will raze this tower."«
8.10: Zebeah and Salmana were at Carchor and their army with them, about fifteen thousand men, all those who had remained of the whole army of the sons of the East, for one hundred and twenty thousand men had fallen, drawing the sword.
8.11: Gideon went up by the way of those who dwell in tents, to the east of Nobe and Jegbaa, and he set up camp, thinking himself safe.
8.12: Zebe and Shalman fled, he pursued them and captured the two kings of Midian, Zebe and Shalman, and routed the entire camp.
8.13: Gideon, son of Joash, returned from the battle by way of the ascent of Hares.
8.14: Having seized a young man from among the people of Soccoth, he questioned him and the young man wrote down for him the names of the leaders and elders of Soccoth, seventy-seven men.
8.15: Then Gideon came to the people of Zoccoth and said, «Here are Zebe and Salman, about whom you insulted me, saying, »Is the hand of Zebe and Salman already in your hand that we should give bread to your weary people?’”
8.16: He seized the elders of the city and, having taken thorns from the desert and briers, he punished the people of Soccoth.
8.17: He also razed the tower of Phanuel and killed the men of the city.
8.18: He said to Zebaeus and Salmana, "What were the men you killed on Tabor like?" They replied, "They were like you; each of them looked like a king's son."«
8.19: He said, "They were my brothers, my mother's sons: as the Lord lives, if you had let them live, I would not have killed you."«
8.20: And he said to Jether, his firstborn, "Get up, kill them." But the young man did not draw his sword, because he was afraid, for he was still a young boy.
8.21: Zebeah and Salmanah said, "Get up and attack us, for as the man is, so is his strength." Gideon got up and killed Zebeah and Salmanah, and he took the crescents that were on the necks of their camels.
8.22: The men of Israel said to Gideon, «Reign over us, you and your son and your son’s son, because you have delivered us from the hand of Midian.»
8.23: Gideon said to them, «I will not rule over you, nor will my son rule over you; the Lord will rule over you.»
8.24: Gideon said to them, "I have a request to make of you: give me each of the rings from your plunder." The enemies had gold rings, for they were Ishmaelites.
8.25: They said, "We will gladly give them." And they spread out a cloak, on which each man threw the rings of his plunder.
8.26: The weight of the gold rings that Gideon had requested was one thousand seven hundred shekels of gold, not counting the crescents, the earrings and the purple garments worn by the kings of Midian and not counting the collars that were on the necks of their camels.
8.27: With this gold, Gideon made an ephod and deposited it in his city, Ephrath. All Israel went there to prostitute themselves, and it became a snare for Gideon and his household.
8.28: Midian was humiliated before the children of Israel and he did not lift up his head again, and the land had rest for forty years in the days of Gideon.
8.29: Jerobaal, son of Joash, returned home and stayed in his house.
8.30: Gideon had seventy sons, from him, for he had many wives.
8.31: His concubine, who was in Shechem, also bore him a son who received his from Abimelech.
8.32: Gideon, son of Joash, died at a good old age and was buried in the tomb of Joash, his father, in Ephrah of Abiezer.
8.33: When Gideon died, the children of Israel again prostituted themselves to the Baals and they took Baal-Berith as their god.
8.34: The children of Israel no longer remembered the Lord their God, who had delivered them from the hand of all their enemies around them.
8.35: and they showed no loyalty to the house of Jerobaal-Gideon, in spite of all the good he had done for Israel.

9.1: Abimelech, son of Jerobaal, went to Shechem to his mother's brothers and addressed these words to them and to all the family of his mother's father's house:
9.2: «Please tell all the inhabitants of Shechem: Which is better for you, that seventy men, all sons of Jerobaal, rule over you, or that one man rule over you? Remember that I am your own flesh and blood.»
9.3: When his mother's brothers repeated all these words about him to all the inhabitants of Shechem, their hearts inclined toward Abimelech, for they said to one another, "He is our brother."«
9.4: They gave him seventy shekels of silver, taken from the house of Baal-Berith, and Abimelech used it to bribe worthless people and adventurers, who attached themselves to him.
9.5: He came to his father’s house in Ephrah and killed his brothers, the sons of Jerobaal, seventy of them, on one stone; only Jotham, the youngest son of Jerobaal, escaped because he had hidden himself.
9.6: Then all the inhabitants of Shechem and all the house of Melo gathered together, they came and proclaimed Abimelech king, near the terebinth tree of the monument that is in Shechem.
9.7: When Jotham was informed of this, he went and stood on the summit of Mount Gerizim and, raising his voice, he cried out to them, saying: «Listen to me, inhabitants of Shechem, so that God may listen to you.
9.8: The trees set out to anoint a king to rule over them. They said to the olive tree: Reign over us.
9.9: But the olive tree said to them, “Should I give up my oil, which brings me glory before God and men, to go and sway above the other trees?”
9.10: And the trees said to the fig tree: Come, you, reign over us.
9.11: But the fig tree said to them: Should I give up my sweetness and my excellent fruit, to go and sway above the other trees?
9.12: And the trees said to the vine: Come, you, reign over us.
9.13: But the vine said to them, “Should I give up my wine, which brings joy to God and men, to go and sway above the other trees?”
9.14: Then all the trees said to the thorn bush: Come, you, reign over us.
9.15: And the thorn bush said to the trees: If you really want to anoint me for your king, come, trust in my shade; otherwise, let fire come out of the thorn bush and devour the cedars of Lebanon.
9.16: Now, if you acted fairly and justly in making Abimelech king, if you dealt well with Jerobaal and his household, and if you treated him according to the merit of his own hands,
9.17: for my father fought for you, he risked his life and delivered you from the hand of Midian,
9.18: And you have risen up today against my father's house, you have killed his sons, seventy of them, on one stone, and you have made Abimelech, the son of his maidservant, king over the men of Shechem, because he is your brother,
9.19: If you have acted with fairness and justice this day towards Jerobaal and his house, then let Abimelech be your joy and let you be his too.
9.20: Otherwise, let fire come out from Abimelech and consume the inhabitants of Shechem and the house of Melo, and let fire come out from the inhabitants of Shechem and the house of Melo and consume Abimelech.»
9.21: Joatham withdrew and fled, he went to Bera and remained there, for fear of Abimelech, his brother.
9.22: Abimelech ruled over Israel for three years.
9.23: And God sent an evil spirit between Abimelech and the inhabitants of Shechem, and the inhabitants of Shechem became unfaithful to Abimelech.
9.24: so that the crime committed against the seventy sons of Jerobaal might be avenged and their blood might fall upon Abimelech, their brother, who had killed them, and upon the men of Shechem who had helped him to kill his brothers.
9.25: The men of Shechem placed men in ambush against him on the mountaintops, who stripped everyone who passed by them on the road, and this was reported to Abimelech.
9.26: Gaal, son of Obed, came with his brothers and they crossed over to Shechem. The men of Shechem put their trust in him.
9.27: They went out into the countryside, harvested their vineyards, trod the grapes and held a feast, then, having entered the house of their god, they ate and drank and they cursed Abimelech.
9.28: Then Gaal son of Obed said, «Who is Abimelech and who is Shechem, that we should serve him? Is not he the son of Jerobaal, and is not Zebul his officer? Serve the men of Emor, Shechem’s father, but why should we serve Abimelech?”
9.29: "Oh, that I were the leader of this people! I would drive out Abimelech!" And he said to Abimelech, "Strengthen your army and go out."»
9.30: Zebul, the city's governor, heard the words of Gaal, son of Obed, and his anger was kindled.
9.31: He secretly sent messengers to Abimelech, saying, «Look, Gaal son of Obed has come to Shechem with his brothers, and they are stirring up the city against you.
9.32: Rise up in the night, you and the people who are with you, and lie in ambush in the countryside.
9.33: In the morning, at sunrise, get up and rush towards the city, and when Gaal and the people with him come out against you, you will do to them as the opportunity allows.»
9.34: Abimelech and all the people who were with him got up at night and lay in ambush near Shechem, divided into four companies.
9.35: Gaal, son of Obed, went out and stood at the entrance of the city gate. Immediately Abimelech and all the people who were with him rose from the ambush.
9.36: When Gaal saw the people, he said to Zebul, "Look, people are coming down from the top of the mountains." Zebul replied, "You think the shadows of the mountains are people."«
9.37: Gaal spoke again and said, «Here is a troop coming down from the middle of the country, and a band arriving by way of the oak of the diviners.»
9.38: Zebul answered him, «Where is your mouth now, with which you said, »Who is Abimelech, that we should serve him?’ Are they not the people you despised? Now go out and fight them.”
9.39: Gaal led a sortie at the head of Shechem's men and gave battle to Abimelech.
9.40: Abimelech pursued him, and Gaal fled before him, and many of his men fell dead at the entrance of the gate.
9.41: Abimelech stopped at Aruma and Zebul drove out Gaal and his brothers, who could no longer remain in Shechem.
9.42: The next day, the people went out into the countryside. Abimelech having been informed,
9.43: He took his troops, divided them into three corps, and lay in ambush in the countryside. As soon as he saw the people leaving the city, he rose up against them and defeated them.
9.44: Abimelech and the corps that were with him rushed forward and stood at the entrance of the city gate; two of these corps rushed upon all those who were in the countryside and defeated them.
9.45: And Abimelech attacked the city all day long, he captured it and killed the people who were in it, then he razed the city and sowed salt there.
9.46: Upon hearing this news, all the men from the tower of Shechem went to the fortress of the house of the god Berith.
9.47: As soon as Abimelech was informed that all the inhabitants of the tower of Shechem had gathered there,
9.48: Abimelech went up Mount Selmon, he and all the people who were with him. Abimelech took an axe in his hand, cut down a branch from a tree, lifted it up, and put it on his shoulder. He said to the people who were with him, «What you have seen me do, hurry and do as I have done.»
9.49: And all the people also cut off a branch each and followed Abimelech; they placed the branches against the fortress and set it on fire, along with all those inside. And all the people of the tower of Shechem also perished, about a thousand men and women.
9.50: From there, Abimelech marched against Thebes, he besieged Thebes and captured it.
9.51: In the middle of the city there was a strong tower where all the inhabitants of the city, men and women, took refuge; having closed the door behind them, they climbed onto the roof of the tower.
9.52: Abimelech came to the tower, attacked it, and approached the tower door to set it on fire.
9.53: Then a woman threw a piece of millstone onto Abimelech's head and smashed his skull.
9.54: He immediately called to the young man who carried his weapons and said to him, "Draw your sword and kill me, so that it will not be said of me: A woman killed him." The young man pierced him and he died.
9.55: When the men of Israel saw that Abimelech was dead, they each went to their own house.
9.56: So God brought back on Abimelech the evil he had done to his father, by killing his seventy brothers
9.57: And God brought back upon the heads of the people of Shechem all their wickedness. Thus was fulfilled upon them the curse of Jotham, son of Jerobaal.

10.1: After Abimelech, Tola, son of Puah, son of Dodo, a man of Issachar, arose to deliver Israel; he lived in Samir, in the hill country of Ephraim.
10.2: He was a judge in Israel for twenty-three years, then he died and was buried in Samir.
10.3: After him arose Jair of Gilead, who judged Israel for twenty-two years.
10.4: He had thirty sons, who rode thirty donkeys and who owned thirty towns still called today Boroughs of Jair and located in the land of Gilead.
10.5: And Jair died and was buried in Camon.
10.6: The children of Israel again did what was evil in the sight of the Lord; they served the Baals and the Ashtoreths, the gods of Syria, the gods of Sidon, the gods of Moab, the gods of the Ammonites and the gods of the Philistines, and they forsook the Lord and no longer served him.
10.7: The Lord’s anger was kindled against Israel, and he sold them into the hands of the Philistines and into the hands of the Ammonites.
10.8: They oppressed and crushed the children of Israel in that year, and this oppression lasted eighteen years for all the children of Israel who lived on the other side of the Jordan, in the land of the Amorites in Gilead.
10.9: The Ammonites crossed the Jordan to fight against Judah, Benjamin, and the house of Ephraim, and Israel was brought to a great distress.
10.10: The Israelites cried out to the Lord, saying, «We have sinned against you, for we have abandoned our God and served the Baals.»
10.11: The Lord said to the children of Israel, «Did I not deliver you from the Egyptians, the Amorites, the Ammonites, and the Philistines?
10.12: And when the Sidonians, Amalek, and Maon oppressed you, and you cried out to me, did I not save you from their hands?
10.13: But you have abandoned me and served other gods; therefore I will no longer deliver you.
10.14: Go, invoke the gods you have chosen, that they may deliver you in your time of distress.»
10.15: The children of Israel said to the Lord, «We have sinned; do to us all as you see fit. Only deign to deliver us this day.»
10.16: And they removed the foreign gods from among them and served the Lord, and his soul could not bear the sufferings of Israel.
10.17: The Ammonites gathered together and camped in Gilead, and the Israelites gathered together and camped at Maspha.
10.18: The people, the leaders of Gilead, said to one another, "Who is the man who will begin the attack against the Ammonites? He will become leader of all the inhabitants of Gilead."«

11.1: Jephthah the Gileadite was a valiant warrior. He was the son of a courtesan, and it was Gilead who fathered Jephthah.
11.2: Gilead's wife bore him sons, and her sons grew up and drove Jephthah out, saying to him, "You will not inherit in our father's house, because you are the son of another woman."«
11.3: And Jephthah fled from his brothers and lived in the land of Tob. Some scoundrels gathered around Jephthah and went on raids with him.
11.4: Some time later, the sons of Ammon waged war against Israel.
11.5: While the Ammonites were at war with Israel, the elders of Gilead went to get Jephthah from the land of Tob.
11.6: They said to Jephthah, "Come, you will be our general and we will fight the Ammonites."«
11.7: Jephthah said to the elders of Gilead, «Did you not hate me and drive me out of my father’s house? Why then do you come to me now that you are in distress?»
11.8: The elders of Gilead said to Jephthah, "That is why we are now returning to you, so that you may march with us, fight against the Ammonites, and be our leader, the leader of all the inhabitants of Gilead."«
11.9: Jephthah answered the elders of Gilead, «If you bring me back to fight against the Ammonites and the Lord delivers them into my hands, I will be your leader.»
11.10: The elders of Gilead said to Jephthah, "May the Lord be our witness: we will certainly do as you say."«
11.11: And Jephthah went with the elders of Gilead. The people appointed him as their leader and general, and Jephthah repeated all his words before the Lord at Maspha.
11.12: Jephthah sent messengers to the king of the Ammonites, saying, «What have you to do with me, that you have come against me to wage war against my country?»
11.13: The king of the Ammonites answered Jephthah's messengers, "Because Israel, when they came up out of Egypt, took possession of my land, from the Arnon to the Jabbok and the Jordan. Now give it back willingly."«
11.14: Jephthah again sent messengers to the king of the Ammonites
11.15: and he said to him, «Thus says Jephthah: Israel has not taken possession of the land of Moab, nor of the land of the sons of Ammon.
11.16: For when Israel went up from Egypt, they walked in the desert as far as the Red Sea and they arrived at Kadesh.
11.17: So Israel sent messengers to the king of Edom, saying, “Please let us pass through your country,” but the king of Edom refused. They also sent messengers to the king of Moab, who likewise refused, and Israel remained in Kadesh.
11.18: Then, marching through the desert, he went around the land of Edom and the land of Moab and came to the east of the land of Moab. And they camped beyond the Arnon and did not come to the border of Moab, for the Arnon is the border of Moab.
11.19: From there, Israel sent messengers to Sihon, king of the Amorites, king of Heshbon, and Israel said to him, “Please let us pass through your land to our place.”.
11.20: But Sihon did not trust Israel enough to let them pass through his territory. Sihon gathered all his people, they camped at Jasa, and he fought against Israel.
11.21: And the Lord, the God of Israel, delivered Sihon and all his people into the hands of Israel, who defeated them, and Israel took possession of all the land of the Amorites who lived in that region.,
11.22: They took possession of all the territory of the Amorites, from the Arnon to the Jaboc and from the desert to the Jordan.
11.23: Now that the Lord, the God of Israel, has driven out the Amorites before his people Israel, you would drive them out.
11.24: That which your God Shamash has given you as a possession, do you not possess it? And all that the Lord our God has put before us as a possession, we would not possess it.
11.25: Are you then better than Balak, son of Zippor, king of Moab? Did he contend with Israel, or did he wage war against them?
11.26: For three hundred years Israel has lived in Heshbon and its dependencies, in Aroer and its dependencies, and in all the towns along the Arnon River. Why have you not taken them away from them during that time?
11.27: »I have not sinned against you, but you have wronged me by waging war against me. May the Lord, the Supreme Judge, judge today between the children of Israel and the children of Ammon.”
11.28: The king of the Ammonites did not listen to the words that Jephthah had sent him to say.
11.29: The Spirit of the Lord came upon Jephthah. He passed through Gilead and Manasseh and went on to Maspha of Gilead, and from Maspha of Gilead he marched against the Ammonites.
11.30: Jephthah made a vow to the Lord, saying:
11.31: «If you deliver the Ammonites into my hands, then whoever comes out of the gates of my house to meet me when I return safely from the Ammonites will belong to the Lord, and I will offer him as a burnt offering.»
11.32: Jephthah advanced against the Ammonites, and the Lord delivered them into his hands.
11.33: He defeated them from Aroer to Mennith, capturing twenty of their cities, and as far as Abel-keramin; it was a very great defeat. And the Ammonites were subdued before the Israelites.
11.34: Jephthah went back up to his house in Maspha, and behold, his daughter came out to meet him with tambourines and dancing. She was his only child; apart from her, he had neither son nor daughter.
11.35: As soon as he saw her, he tore his clothes and said, «Ah, my daughter, you overwhelm me and are among those who trouble me. I have opened my mouth to the Lord, and I cannot turn back.»
11.36: She said to him, «My father, you have opened your mouth to the Lord; do to me according to what has come out of your mouth, since the Lord has avenged you on your enemies, the Ammonites.»
11.37: And she said to her father, "Grant me this favor only, let me go free for two months, I will leave, I will go down to the mountains and I will mourn my virginity with my companions."«
11.38: He replied, "Go," and he let her go for two months. She and her companions went away and wept over her virginity on the mountains.
11.39: Two months later, she returned to her father, and he fulfilled the vow he had made to her, and she had not known a man. From this came the custom in Israel:
11.40: Each year the daughters of Israel celebrate the daughter of Jephthah the Gileadite for four days a year.

12.1: The men of Ephraim gathered together, passed by Zaphon, and said to Jephthah, «Why did you go to fight against the Ammonites without calling us to go with you? We will burn your house down over you.»
12.2: Jephthah answered them, «I and my people were in great conflict with the Ammonites, so I called you, but you did not deliver me from their hands.
12.3: Seeing that you did not come to my aid, I risked my life and marched against the Ammonites, and the Lord delivered them into my hands. Why then have you come up against me today to wage war against me?»
12.4: Jephthah gathered all the men of Gilead and went into battle against Ephraim. The men of Gilead defeated those of Ephraim, for the latter had said, «You Gileadites are nothing but fugitives from Ephraim, in the midst of Ephraim and Manasseh.»
12.5: Gilead seized the fords of the Jordan on the side of Ephraim, and when one of the fleeing Ephraims said, "Let me pass," the men of Gilead asked him, "Are you an Ephraimite?" He answered, "No.".
12.6: They would tell him, «Say »Shibboleth.«» But he would say, “Sibboleth,” though he could not pronounce it correctly. So they seized him and slaughtered him at the fords of the Jordan. At that time, forty-two thousand Ephraimites died.
12.7: Jephthah judged Israel for six years, then Jephthah the Gileadite died and was buried in one of the cities of Gilead.
12.8: After him, Abesan of Bethlehem became a judge in Israel.
12.9: He had thirty sons, he married thirty daughters outside his household, and he brought thirty daughters from elsewhere for his sons. He judged Israel for seven years,
12.10: Then Abesan died and was buried in Bethlehem.
12.11: After him, Ahilon of Zebulun was a judge in Israel; he was a judge in Israel for ten years.,
12.12: Then Ahialon of Zebulun died and was buried in Ahialon in the land of Zebulun.
12.13: After him, Abdon, son of Ilel, of Pharathon, was judge in Israel.
12.14: He had forty sons and thirty grandsons, who rode on seventy donkeys. He served as a judge in Israel for eight years.,
12.15: Then Abdon, son of Ilel, of Pharathon, died and was buried in Pharathon, in the land of Ephraim, on the mountain of the Amalekites.

13.1: The children of Israel again did what was evil in the sight of the Lord, and the Lord delivered them into the hands of the Philistines for forty years.
13.2: There was a man from Saraa, of the family of the Danites, named Manué; his wife was barren and had not borne children.
13.3: The Angel of the Lord appeared to the woman and said to her, «Behold, you are barren and childless, but you will conceive and bear a son.
13.4: And now, be very careful, drink neither wine nor strong drink and eat nothing unclean,
13.5: For you will conceive and give birth to a son. No razor will touch his head, for he will be a Nazirite, a member of God from his mother’s womb, and he will begin to deliver Israel from the hand of the Philistines.»
13.6: The woman went and told her husband, «A man from God came to me. He had the appearance of an angel of God, and he was very awesome. I did not ask him where he was from, and he did not tell me his name.”
13.7: But he said to me, «You will conceive and give birth to a son, and now drink no wine or strong drink, and eat nothing unclean, because this child will be a Nazirite of God from the womb until the day of his death.»
13.8: Then Manué called upon the Lord and said, «I pray to you, Lord, that the man of God whom you have sent may come to us again and teach us what we should do for the child who will be born.»
13.9: God answered Manué's prayer and the Angel of God came again to the woman, she was sitting in a field and Manué, her husband, was not with her.
13.10: The woman immediately ran to inform her husband and said, "Look, the man who came to me the other day has appeared to me."«
13.11: Manué got up and, following his wife, went to the man and said to him, "Were you the one who spoke to this woman?" He replied, "It was me."«
13.12: Manué said, «Now, when your word comes true, what must be done for this child, and what must be done for him?»
13.13: The Angel of the Lord answered Manué: «The woman must abstain from everything I have told her:
13.14: She shall eat nothing that comes from the vine, she shall drink neither wine nor strong drink, and she shall eat nothing unclean: all that I have commanded her, she shall observe.»
13.15: Manué said to the Angel of the Lord, «Please let us detain you and prepare a young goat for you.»
13.16: The Angel of the Lord answered Manué, «Even if you detain me, I will not eat your food; but if you wish to prepare a burnt offering, offer it to the Lord.» Manué did not know that it was the Angel of the Lord.
13.17: And Manué said to the Angel of the Lord, «What is your name, so that we may honor you when your word comes true?»
13.18: The Angel of the Lord answered him, «Why do you ask me about my name? It is wonderful.»
13.19: Manué took the kid with the offering and offered it to the Lord on the rock, and the Lord performed a miracle while Manué and his wife watched.
13.20: As the flame rose from above the altar to heaven, the Angel of the Lord ascended in the flame of the altar. At this sight, Manué and his wife fell facedown to the ground.
13.21: And the Angel of the Lord did not appear again to Manué and his wife. Then Manué understood that it was the Angel of the Lord.
13.22: And Manué said to his wife, "We are going to die, for we have seen God."«
13.23: His wife replied, "If the Lord had wanted us to die, he would not have accepted the burnt offering and the grain offering from our hands, he would not have shown us all this, he would not have told us such things today."«
13.24: The woman gave birth to a son and named him Samson. The child grew up and the Lord blessed him.,
13.25: the spirit of the Lord began to drive him to Machaneh-Dan, between Saraa and Esthaol.

14.1: Samson went down to Thamna and he saw in Thamna a woman from among the daughters of the Philistines.
14.2: When he had gone up, he told his father and mother, saying, «I have seen in Thamna a woman from among the daughters of the Philistines; now take her to be my wife.»
14.3: His father and mother said to him, «Is there no woman among the daughters of your brothers or in all our people, that you must go and take a wife from the uncircumcised Philistines?» And Samson said to his father, «Get her for me, for she pleases me.»
14.4: His father and mother did not know that this was from the Lord, for he was looking for an opportunity to quarrel with the Philistines. At that time the Philistines ruled over Israel.
14.5: Samson went down with his father and mother to Thamna. When they arrived at the vineyards of Thamna, behold, a roaring young lion came to meet him.
14.6: The Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon Samson, and with nothing in his hand, Samson tore the lion apart as one tears a young goat. And he did not tell his father or mother what he had done.
14.7: He went downstairs and spoke to the woman, and she pleased him.
14.8: Some time later, having gone back to Thamna to take it, he made a detour to see the lion's corpse and behold, there was a swarm of bees and honey in the lion's body.
14.9: He took some in his hands and ate it as he went along, and when he came to his father and mother, he gave some to them and they ate it, but he did not tell them that he had taken the honey from the lion's body.
14.10: Samson's father went down to the woman's house and there Samson gave a feast, for that was the custom of young men.
14.11: As soon as they saw him, they invited thirty companions to be with him.
14.12: Samson said to them, «I will give you a riddle. If you explain it to me during the seven days of the feast and solve it, I will give you thirty tunics and thirty changes of clothing,
14.13: But if you cannot explain it to me, you will give me thirty tunics and thirty changes of clothing.» They said to him, «Tell us your riddle, so that we may hear it.»
14.14: He told them, "Out of the eater came something to be eaten, and out of the strong came something sweet." For three days they could not explain the riddle.
14.15: On the seventh day, they said to Samson's wife, "Persuade your husband to explain the riddle to us, or we will burn you and your father's house. Is it to rob us that you invited us?"«
14.16: Samson's wife wept beside him and said, "You hate me and do not love me. You have posed a riddle to the sons of my people, but you have not explained it to me." He replied, "I have not explained it to my father or my mother, but I will explain it to you."«
14.17: She wept before him for the seven days that the feast lasted; on the seventh day, as she tormented him, he explained the riddle to her, and she explained it to the children of her people.
14.18: The people of the town said to Samson on the seventh day, before sunset, «What is sweeter than honey, and what is stronger than a lion?» And he said to them, «If you had not plowed with my heifer, you would not have solved my riddle.»
14.19: The Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon him, and he went down to Ascalon. There he killed thirty men and, having taken their spoils, gave the changes of clothing to those who had solved the riddle. Then, burning with anger, he went up to his father's house.
14.20: Samson's wife was given to one of his companions whom he had chosen as a friend.

15.1: Some time later, at harvest time, Samson went to see his wife, bringing a young goat. He said, «I want to go to my wife’s room.» But his father would not let him in.
15.2: And her father said, "I thought you hated her, so I gave her to your friend. Isn't her younger sister more beautiful than she? Let her be your wife instead."«
15.3: Samson told them, "This time I will be innocent before the Philistines if I harm them."«
15.4: And Samson went away. He caught three hundred foxes and, having taken torches, he turned the foxes tail to tail and put a torch between the two tails, in the middle.
15.5: He then lit the torches and released the foxes into the Philistines' crops, setting fire to everything from the sheaves of grain to the standing wheat and the olive groves.
15.6: The Philistines said, "Who did this?" They answered, "Samson, the son-in-law of the Thamnean, because he took his wife and gave her to his friend." And the Philistines went up and burned her and her father.
15.7: Samson said to them, "Is this how you act? Well, I will not stop until I have taken my revenge on you."«
15.8: And he made a great slaughter of them, breaking their thighs and hips, then he went down and remained in the cavern of the Etam rock.
15.9: Then the Philistines went up and camped in Judah, spreading out into Lehi.
15.10: The men of Judah said, «Why have you turned against us?» They replied, «We have come up to bind Samson, so that we may treat him as he has treated us.»
15.11: Three thousand men of Judah went down to the cave in the rock of Etam and said to Samson, «Don’t you know that the Philistines are our masters? What have you done to us?» He answered them, «I treated them as they treated me.»
15.12: They told him, "We have come down to bind you, so that we may hand you over to the Philistines." Samson said to them, "Swear to me that you will not kill me."«
15.13: They answered him, saying, "No, we only want to bind you and hand you over to them, but we will not kill you." And having bound him with two new ropes, they brought him up from the rock.
15.14: When he arrived at Lehi, the Philistines shouted for joy to meet him. Then the Spirit of the Lord came powerfully upon him, and the cords on his arms became like flax that has been burned with fire, and his bonds fell off his hands.
15.15: Finding a fresh donkey jawbone, he stretched out his hand, seized it, and struck a thousand men with it.
15.16: And Samson said, "With a donkey's jawbone, I thrashed them well; with a donkey's jawbone, I struck a thousand men."«
15.17: When he had finished speaking, he threw down his jawbone and named the place Ramath-Lehi.
15.18: Consumed by thirst, he called upon the Lord and said, «It is you who have granted this great deliverance through the hand of your servant, and now must I die of thirst and fall into the hands of the uncircumcised?»
15.19: And God split open the hollow rock that is at Lehi, and water came out. Samson drank, his spirit revived, and he came back to life. That is why the spring was called En-Hakkore; it exists at Lehi to this day.
15.20: Samson judged Israel for twenty years during the time of the Philistines.

16.1: Samson went to Gaza, saw a courtesan there, and entered her house.
16.2: The news was announced to the people of Gaza, saying, «Samson has come here.» And they surrounded him and lay in wait for him all night at the city gate. They remained quiet all night, saying, «Let’s wait until morning and then we’ll kill him.»
16.3: Samson remained lying down until midnight, at midnight he got up and, taking hold of the doors of the city gate and the two posts, he tore them down with the bar, put them on his shoulders and carried them to the top of the mountain that overlooks Hebron.
16.4: After that, he fell in love with a woman in the valley of Sorec, her name was Dalila.
16.5: The Philistine princes went up to her and said, «Flatter him and see where his great strength lies and how we can overpower him, bind him, and subdue him, and we will each give you one thousand and one hundred shekels of silver.»
16.6: Delilah said to Samson, "Tell me, I pray you, where does your great strength lie, and with what must I bind you to subdue you?"«
16.7: Samson said to him, "If I were bound with seven fresh ropes that were not yet dry, I would become weak and be like any other man."«
16.8: The Philistine princes brought Delilah seven fresh ropes, which were not yet dry, and she bound him with these ropes.
16.9: But she had men lying in ambush in the room. She said to him, "The Philistines are upon you, Samson." And he broke the ropes, as a cord of tow breaks when it feels the fire, and the secret of his strength was not known.
16.10: Delilah said to Samson, "Look, you have mocked me and told me lies. Now please tell me how I should bind you."«
16.11: He told him, "If I were bound with new ropes that had never been used, I would become weak and be like any other man."«
16.12: Delilah took new ropes and bound him with them. Then she said to him, «The Philistines are upon you, Samson.» Now some men were lying in wait in the room, and he snapped the ropes that were on his arms like a thread.
16.13: Delilah said to Samson, "So far you have deceived me and lied to me. Tell me how I should bind you." He said to her, "Just weave the seven braids of my head with the cloth."«
16.14: And she fastened them with the peg. Then she said to him, "The Philistines are upon you, Samson." And, waking from his sleep, he pulled the peg out of the loom and the cloth.
16.15: She said to him, "How can you say 'I love you' when your heart is not with me? This is the third time you have deceived me and failed to show me where your true strength lies."«
16.16: She tormented him thus every day and wearyed him with her entreaties; finally, his soul grew impatient until he died.,
16.17: He opened his heart to her and said, «No razor has ever touched my head, for I have been a Nazirite to God from my mother’s womb. If I were shaved, my strength would leave me, I would become weak, and I would be like all other men.»
16.18: When Delilah saw that he had told her everything, she sent word to the Philistine princes, saying, «Come up this time, for he has told me everything.» So the Philistine princes came up to her, bringing the money in their hands.
16.19: She lulled him to sleep on her knees and, having called the man, she shaved off the seven braids of Samson's head and began to subdue him, and his strength departed from him.
16.20: She then said, "The Philistines are upon you, Samson." He awoke from his sleep and said, "I will get out of this as at other times and free myself," for he did not know that the Lord had departed from him.
16.21: The Philistines seized him and gouged out his eyes, and having brought him down to Gaza, they bound him with a double bronze chain. He was grinding in the prison.
16.22: However, the hair on his head had begun to grow back since he had been shaved.
16.23: The Philistine princes gathered to offer a great sacrifice to Dagon, their god, and to rejoice. They said, "Our god has delivered Samson, our enemy, into our hands."«
16.24: The people saw it and they praised their god, for they said, "Our god has delivered into our hands our enemy, the one who ravaged our land and killed so many of us."«
16.25: When their hearts were filled with joy, they said, «Bring Samson here to entertain us.» So they brought Samson out of prison, and he danced before them. He was placed between the pillars.
16.26: Samson said to the young man who was holding his hand: "Let me touch the pillars on which the house stands and lean against them.
16.27: Now the house was full of men and women, all the princes of the Philistines were there, and there were about three thousand people on the roof, men and women, watching Samson dance.
16.28: Then Samson called upon the Lord and said, «Lord God, remember me, I pray, and give me strength just this once, O God, so that with one blow I may take revenge on the Philistines for my two eyes.»
16.29: And Samson, embracing the two middle pillars on which the house stood, leaned on them, on one with his right hand, on the other with his left hand.
16.30: And Samson said, «Let me die with the Philistines!» And he bent down with great force, and the house collapsed on the princes and all the people who were in it. Those whom he killed when he died were more than those whom he had killed during his life.
16.31: His brothers and all his father's household went down to Gaza and brought him back. When they returned, they buried him between Sarah and Eshtaol, in the tomb of Manuah, his father. He had judged Israel for twenty years.

17.1: There was a man from the hill country of Ephraim, named Micah.
17.100: shekels of silver to his mother, and his mother said, "I dedicate this silver to the Lord for my son, to make a carved image and a cast object, and now I give it back to you."«
17.4: When he had returned the money to his mother, his mother took two hundred shekels and gave them to the metalworker, who made a carved image and a cast object, and they were in the house of Micah.
17.5: For this Micah had a house of God, he made an ephod and teraphim and he consecrated one of his sons, who served him as priest.
17.6: At that time there was no king in Israel; everyone did what was right in their own eyes.
17.7: There was a young man from Bethlehem of Judah, of the family of Judah, he was a Levite and was staying in that city.
17.8: This man left the city of Bethlehem in Judah, to find a place to stay. He thus arrived in the hill country of Ephraim, as far as the house of Micah.
17.9: Micah asked him, "Where are you from?" He replied, "I am a Levite from Bethlehem in Judah, and I am traveling to find a place to stay."«
17.10: Michas said to him, «Stay with me, be a father and a priest to me, and I will give you ten shekels of silver a year, a supply of clothing and food.» And the Levite went in.
17.11: The Levite agreed to stay with this man, and the young man was like one of his sons to him.
17.12: Michas installed the Levite and the young man served as his priest and remained in Michas's house.
17.13: And Michas said, «Now I know that the Lord will do me good, since I have this Levite as my priest.»

18.1: At that time there was no king in Israel. At that time the tribe of Dan was seeking a possession to settle on, for until that day no inheritance had fallen to them among the tribes of Israel.
18.2: The sons of Dan, having taken five valiant men from their families, sent them from Sarah and Eshtaol to spy out the land and explore it. They told them, «Go, explore the land.» The five men arrived in the hill country of Ephraim as far as the house of Micah and spent the night there.
18.3: As they were near the house of Michas, recognizing the voice of the young Levite, they approached him and said, «Who brought you here? What are you doing in this place and what do you have here?»
18.4: He replied to them, "Michas has done such and such for me, he gives me a salary, and I serve as his priest."«
18.5: They told him, "Well, consult God, so that we may know whether our journey will be successful."«
18.6: And the priest replied to them, "Go in peace, the journey you are making is under the Lord's gaze."«
18.7: Having set out, the five men arrived at Laish. They saw the people who were in that city, living securely in the manner of the Sidonians, peaceful and quiet, and there was no one in the country who, invested with authority, harassed them in any way; they were far from the Sidonians and had no dealings with anyone.
18.8: They returned to their brothers at Sarah and Eshtaol, and their brothers said to them, "What do you say?"«
18.9: They replied, «Get up and let us go up against them, for we have seen the land, and behold, it is very good. And you are standing here without saying a word? Do not be lazy, but set out and go and take possession of this land.”.
18.10: Upon entering, you will arrive among a people in safety. The land is vast, and God has given it into your hands; it is a place where nothing is lacking of all that is on earth.»
18.11: Six hundred men from the family of Dan set out from Saraa and Esthaol, armed with their weapons of war.
18.12: They went up and camped at Cariathariam in Judah, therefore that place has been called Machaneh-Dan to this day, behold, it is west of Cariathariam.
18.13: From there they went into the hill country of Ephraim and came to the house of Micah.
18.14: The five men who had been exploring the land of Laish spoke up and said to their brothers, «Do you know that in these houses there is an ephod, teraphim, a carved image, and a cast object? Now consider what you must do.»
18.15: They went in that direction and, entering the house of the young Levite, the house of Michas, they asked him how he was doing.
18.16: The six hundred men from among the sons of Dan, armed with their weapons of war, stood at the entrance of the gate.
18.17: And the five men who had gone to explore the land went up and, having entered the sanctuary, they took the carved image, the ephod, the teraphim and the cast object, while the priest stood at the entrance of the gate with the six hundred men with their weapons of war.
18.18: When they had entered the house of Micah and had taken the carved image, the ephod, the teraphim, and the cast object, the priest said to them, «What are you doing?»
18.19: They answered him, «Be quiet, put your hand over your mouth, and come with us, and you will be a father and a priest to us. Is it better for you to be a priest for the household of one man, or for a tribe and a clan in Israel?»
18.20: The priest's heart became joyful, he took the ephod, the teraphim and the carved image and came into the midst of the company.
18.21: They set out again and left, having placed before them the children, the livestock, and all the valuables.
18.22: They were already far from Michas' house when the people who lived in the houses near Michas' house gathered together and pursued the sons of Dan.
18.23: They shouted at the sons of Dan, and the sons turned around and said to Micah, "What is this you want, that you have gathered these men together?"«
18.24: He replied, "My gods that I made, you have taken them away, along with the priest, and you have left: what do I have left? How then can you say to me: What do you need?"«
18.25: The sons of Dan said to him, «Do not let us hear your voice, lest angry men attack you and you lose your life and the lives of your household.»
18.26: And Dan's sons continued on their way. Seeing that they were stronger than him, Micah turned back and returned to his house.
18.27: So the Danites took away what Micah had made and the priest who was in his service, and they marched against Laish, against a people at peace and secure, they put them to the edge of the sword and burned the city.
18.28: There was no one to deliver it, for it was far from Sidon and its inhabitants did not deal with other men: it was in the valley that stretches toward Beth-rohob. The sons of Dan rebuilt the city and lived there,
18.29: They called it Dan, after the name of Dan, their father, who was born of Israel, but the city was originally called Laish.
18.30: The sons of Dan set up the carved image for them, and Jonathan, son of Gersam, son of Moses, he and his sons were priests of the tribe of Dan until the day of the captivity of the land.
18.31: They set up for themselves the carved image that Micah had made, during all the time that the house of God was in Shiloh.

19.1: At that time, when there was no king in Israel, a Levite, who was staying at the edge of the hill country of Ephraim, took as his concubine a woman from Bethlehem in Judah.
19.2: His concubine was unfaithful to him and she left him to go to her father's house in Bethlehem of Judah, where she stayed for four months.
19.3: Her husband got up and went to her, to speak tenderly to her and bring her back to him. He had with him his servant and two donkeys. She brought him into her father's house, and when the young woman's father saw him, he went joyfully to meet him.
19.4: His stepfather, the young woman's father, kept him there and he stayed at his house for three days; they ate and drank and they stayed there.
19.5: On the fourth day, they got up early in the morning and the Levite was preparing to leave. But the young woman's father said to his son-in-law, "Strengthen your heart by taking a piece of bread and then you can leave."«
19.6: Having sat down, they both ate and drank together. Then the young woman's father said to the husband, "Please agree to spend the night and let your heart rejoice."«
19.7: The husband got up to leave, but, at the urging of his father-in-law, he returned and spent another night there.
19.8: On the fifth day, he got up early in the morning to leave. Then the young woman's father said, "Strengthen your heart, I beg you, and delay until the end of the day." And they both ate.
19.9: The husband was getting up to leave, he, his concubine, and his servant, but his father-in-law, the young woman's father, said to him, "Look, the day is drawing to a close; please stay here all night. The day is almost over; stay here and be glad. Tomorrow you will get up early and go to your tent."«
19.10: The husband did not agree to spend the night; he got up and left. He came as far as Jebus, which is Jerusalem, with the two donkeys with their saddles and his concubine.
19.11: When they approached Jebus, the day was far over. The servant then said to his master, «Come, please, let us turn aside and go to this city of the Jebusites and spend the night there.»
19.12: His master replied, «We will not turn aside to a foreign city where there are no Israelites; we will go on to Gibeah.»
19.13: He also said to his servant, "Come, let us try to reach one of these places to spend the night, either Gabaa or Rama."«
19.14: They continued walking and the sun set when they were near Gabaah, which belongs to Benjamin.
19.15: They turned off in that direction to spend the night at Gibeah. The Levite, having entered, stopped in the city square, and there was no one who received them into his house to spend the night.
19.16: But behold, an old man was returning in the evening from working in the fields; he was a man from the hill country of Ephraim, who was staying in Gibeah, and the people of the place were Benjamites.
19.17: Looking up, he saw the traveler in the town square and the old man said, "Where are you going and where are you coming from?"«
19.18: He answered him, «We are going from Bethlehem of Judah to the far side of the hill country of Ephraim, where I am from. I had gone to Bethlehem of Judah, and now I am going to the house of the Lord, and there is no one who receives me in his house.”.
19.19: However, we have straw and fodder for our donkeys, and also bread and wine for myself, your maid, and the young man who is with your servants; we lack nothing.»
19.20: The old man said, "Peace be with you. I will provide for all your needs, but do not spend the night in the square."«
19.21: He brought him into his house and gave fodder to the donkeys, the travelers washed their feet, then they ate and drank.
19.22: While they were rejoicing, some wicked men from the city surrounded the house and, striking loudly at the door, said to the old man, the master of the house, «Bring out the man who came into your house, so that we may have relations with him.»
